subject: Physics
q: Two projectiles are projected with the same initial velocities at the $15^\circ$ and $30^\circ$ with respect to the horizontal. The ratio of their ranges is $1:x$. The value of $x$ is:
question: Two projectiles are projected with the same initial velocities at the $15^\circ$ and $30^\circ$ with respect to the horizontal. The ratio of their ranges is $1:x$. The value of $x$ is:
solution:
The horizontal range of a projectile is given by the formula $R = \dfrac{u^2 \sin(2\theta)}{g}$.

For the first projectile, the angle of projection is $\theta_1 = 15^\circ$.

$R_1 = \dfrac{u^2 \sin(2 \times 15^\circ)}{g} = \dfrac{u^2 \sin(30^\circ)}{g} = \dfrac{u^2}{2g}$

For the second projectile, the angle of projection is $\theta_2 = 30^\circ$.

$R_2 = \dfrac{u^2 \sin(2 \times 30^\circ)}{g} = \dfrac{u^2 \sin(60^\circ)}{g} = \dfrac{\sqrt{3}u^2}{2g}$

The ratio of their ranges is:

$\dfrac{R_1}{R_2} = \dfrac{\dfrac{u^2}{2g}}{\dfrac{\sqrt{3}u^2}{2g}} = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{3}}$

Given that the ratio is $1:x$, we get $x = \sqrt{3}$.

Answer: $\sqrt{3}$

subject: Physics
q: Two projectiles are projected at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mo>°</mo></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mo>°</mo></math>with the horizontal with the same speed. The ratio of the maximum height attained by the two projectiles respectively is:
question: Two projectiles are projected at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mo>°</mo></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mo>°</mo></math>with the horizontal with the same speed. The ratio of the maximum height attained by the two projectiles respectively is:
solution:
The maximum height is given by, H=u2sin2θ2g.

The given data is 

θ1=30°θ2=60°u1=u2

Therefore,

H1maxH2max=u12sin2θ1u22sin2θ2=sin30°sin60°2=12322=13

subject: Physics
q:
The co-ordinates of a particle moving in $x-y$ plane are given by : $x=2+4 \mathrm{t}, y=3 \mathrm{t}+8 \mathrm{t}^2$.
The motion of the particle is :
question:
The co-ordinates of a particle moving in $x-y$ plane are given by : $x=2+4 \mathrm{t}, y=3 \mathrm{t}+8 \mathrm{t}^2$.
The motion of the particle is :
options: uniformly accelerated having motion along a parabolic path.
uniform motion along a straight line.
uniformly accelerated having motion along a straight line.
solution:
$\begin{aligned} & x=2+4 t \\ & \frac{d x}{d t}=v_x=4 \\ & \frac{d v_x}{d t}=a_x=0 \\ & y=3 t+8 t^2 \\ & \frac{d y}{d t}=v_y=3+16 t \\ & \frac{d v_y}{d t}=a_y=16 \end{aligned}$ the motion will be uniformly accelerated motion. For path $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{x}=2+4 \mathrm{t} \\ & \frac{(\mathrm{x}-2)}{4}=\mathrm{t} \end{aligned}$
Put this value of $t$ is equation of $y$ $y=3\left(\frac{x-2}{4}\right)+8\left(\frac{x-2}{4}\right)^2$ this is a quadratic equation so path will be parabola.

subject: Physics
q: A helicopter is flying horizontally with a speed <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>v</mi></math> at an altitude <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>h</mi></math> has to drop a food packet for a man on the ground. What is the distance of helicopter from the man when the food packet is dropped ?
question: A helicopter is flying horizontally with a speed <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>v</mi></math> at an altitude <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>h</mi></math> has to drop a food packet for a man on the ground. What is the distance of helicopter from the man when the food packet is dropped ?
solution:
The aeroplane imparts v velocity to the food packet horizontally when dropping. Horizontal range of food packet =v×2hg
Also, Horizontal distance, travelled by aeroplane =v×2hg thus, vertical distance between aeroplane and person =h
distance =2v2 h g+h2

The initial velocity of the particle is 52 ms-1 and the air resistance is assumed to be negligible. The magnitude of the change in momentum between the points A and B is x×10-2 kgms-1. The value of x, to the nearest integer, is ___ .
question:
The projectile motion of a particle of mass 5 g is shown in the figure.

The initial velocity of the particle is 52 ms-1 and the air resistance is assumed to be negligible. The magnitude of the change in momentum between the points A and B is x×10-2 kgms-1. The value of x, to the nearest integer, is ___ .
solution:
|u→|=|v→|

u→=ucos45i^+usin45j^

v→=vcos45i^-vsin45j^

|ΔP→|=mv→-u→

ΔP=2musin45°=2×5×10-3×52×12

ΔP=50×10-3=5×10-2.

q:
A particle of mass 100 g is projected at time t=0 with a speed 20 m s–1 at an angle 45° to the horizontal as given in the figure. The magnitude of the angular momentum of the particle about the starting point at time t=2 s is found to be K kg m2 s-1. The value of K is ______.

(Take g=10 m s-2)
question:
A particle of mass 100 g is projected at time t=0 with a speed 20 m s–1 at an angle 45° to the horizontal as given in the figure. The magnitude of the angular momentum of the particle about the starting point at time t=2 s is found to be K kg m2 s-1. The value of K is ______.

(Take g=10 m s-2)
solution:
As torque due to gravitational force will be variable, therefore we can use

 ΔL=∫0tτdt.

Now, torque of mg at time t =vxtmg and horizontal velocity of the projectile will remain constantvx=vcos45°=20×12=102.

L0=∫02mgvxtdt

=mgvxt22=(0.1)(10)(102)222

=202

=800 kg m2 s-1

Therefore, K=800.

q: A ball is spun with angular acceleration <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>6</mn><msup><mi>t</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>t</mi></math> where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi></math> is in second and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi></math> is in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>rad</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>. At <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math>, the ball has angular velocity of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>rad</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> and angular position of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi>rad</mi></math>. The most appropriate expression for the angular position of the ball is
question: A ball is spun with angular acceleration <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>6</mn><msup><mi>t</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>t</mi></math> where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi></math> is in second and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi></math> is in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>rad</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>. At <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math>, the ball has angular velocity of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>rad</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> and angular position of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi>rad</mi></math>. The most appropriate expression for the angular position of the ball is
solution:
Given: α=6t2-2t

Using relation α=dωdt=6t2-2t

Integrating the above, 

∫10ωdω=∫0t6t2-2tdt

⇒ω-10=2t3-t2

Now, ω=dθdt=10+2t3-t2

∫4θdθ=∫0t10+2t3-t2dt

Integrating the above relation, 

θ-4=10t+t42-t33

Thus, angular position of the ball is θ=t42-t33+10t+4.

subject: Physics
q: The maximum height reached by a projectile is $64 \mathrm{~m}$. If the initial velocity is halved, the new maximum height of the projectile is _____ $\mathrm{m}$.
question: The maximum height reached by a projectile is $64 \mathrm{~m}$. If the initial velocity is halved, the new maximum height of the projectile is _____ $\mathrm{m}$.
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{H}_{\max }=\frac{\mathrm{u}^2 \sin ^2 \theta}{2 \mathrm{~g}} \\ & \frac{\mathrm{H}_{1 \text { max }}}{\mathrm{H}_{2 \max }}=\frac{\mathrm{u}_1^2}{\mathrm{u}_2^2} \\ & \frac{64}{\mathrm{H}_{2 \max }}=\frac{\mathrm{u}^2}{(\mathrm{u} / 2)^2} \\ & \mathrm{H}_{2 \max }=16 \mathrm{~m}\end{aligned}$

q: A ball is projected with kinetic energy <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>E</mi></math>, at an angle of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mo>°</mo></math> to the horizontal. The kinetic energy of this ball at the highest point of its flight will become :
question: A ball is projected with kinetic energy <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>E</mi></math>, at an angle of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mo>°</mo></math> to the horizontal. The kinetic energy of this ball at the highest point of its flight will become :
solution:
E=12mu2

At the Highest point, velocity is equal to the horizontal component of the velocity of projection. Therefore,

v=ucos60°=u2

∴ Kinetic energy at the topmost point =12 mu22=E4

question: A shell is fired from a fixed artillery gun with an initial speed u such that it hits the target on the ground at a distance <math><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi></math> from it. If <math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub></math> and <math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></math> are the values of the time taken by it to hit the target in two possible ways, the product <math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></math> is:
solution:
We know for complementary angle range is same for same projection speed.
t1=2usin(θ)g ….. (i)
t2=2usin(90-θ)g ….. (ii)
R=2u2sinθcosθg .… (iii)
From equation (i) and (ii)
t1t2=4 U2sinθ cosθg2
(Using the value of R from equation iii)
t1t2=2Rg

subject: Physics
q: A particle is moving with velocity $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{v}}=\mathrm{K}(\mathrm{y} \hat{\mathrm{i}}+\mathrm{x} \hat{\mathrm{j}})$, where $\mathrm{K}$ is a constant. The general equation for its path is
question: A particle is moving with velocity $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{v}}=\mathrm{K}(\mathrm{y} \hat{\mathrm{i}}+\mathrm{x} \hat{\mathrm{j}})$, where $\mathrm{K}$ is a constant. The general equation for its path is
solution:
$\vec{v}=K y \hat{i}+K x \hat{j}$
$\frac{d x}{d t}=K y, \quad \frac{d y}{d t}=K x$
$\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d y}{d t} \times \frac{d t}{d x}=\frac{K x}{K y}$
$y d y=x d x$
$y^2=x^2+c$

q: Two projectiles are thrown with same initial velocity making an angle of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>45</mn><mo>°</mo></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mo>°</mo></math> with the horizontal respectively. The ratio of their respective ranges will be
question: Two projectiles are thrown with same initial velocity making an angle of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>45</mn><mo>°</mo></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mo>°</mo></math> with the horizontal respectively. The ratio of their respective ranges will be
solution:
Let projection speed is u, The formula for the range of the projectile is given by, R=u2sin2θg. Therefore,

R1=u2sin90°g; R2=u2sin60°g

R1R2=sin90°sin60°=23

After <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math>, its inclination with horizontal is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>β</mi></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>tan</mi><mi>β</mi></math> will be : <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi>g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></mrow></mfenced></math>.
question: A projectile is launched at an angle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi></math> with the horizontal with a velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. After <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math>, its inclination with horizontal is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>β</mi></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>tan</mi><mi>β</mi></math> will be : <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi>g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></mrow></mfenced></math>.
solution:
Component of velocity in horizontal direction,

vx=20cosα=v2cosβ

Component of velocity in vertical direction,

vy=20sinα-g×10=v2sinβ

⇒tanβ=20sinα-10020cosα

⇒tanβ=tanα-5cosα

⇒tanβ=tanα-5secα

question: The position of a projectile launched from the origin at t = 0 is given by <math> <mrow> <mover accent="true"> <mtext>r</mtext> <mo>→</mo> </mover> <mo>=</mo> <mfenced close=")" open="(" separators=","> <mrow> <mn>4</mn> <mn>0 </mn> <mover accent="true"> <mrow> <mi></mi><mi></mi><mi></mi><mi></mi><mi></mi><mi></mi><mi></mi><mi></mi><mi>i</mi><mo>⁡</mo> </mrow> <mo>^</mo> </mover> <mo>+</mo> <mn>5</mn> <mn>0 </mn> <mover accent="true"> <mrow> <mi></mi><mi></mi><mi></mi><mi></mi><mi></mi><mi></mi><mi></mi><mi></mi><mi>j</mi><mo>⁡</mo> </mrow> <mo>^</mo> </mover> </mrow> </mfenced> <mtext>m</mtext> </mrow> </math>  at t = 2s. If the projectile was launched at an angle <math> <mrow> <mi>θ</mi> </mrow> </math> from the horizontal, then <math> <mrow> <mi>θ</mi> </mrow> </math>  is (take g = 10 ms<sup>-2</sup>).
solution:
From question, Horizontal velocity (initial),
ux=402=20 m/s
Vertical velocity (initial), 50=uyt+12gt2

⇒50= uy×2+12(-10)×4
     50=2uy-20
      uy=702=35 m/s
∴  tanθ=uyux=3520=74
⇒  θ=tan-174

subject: Physics
q: A ball of mass 100 g is projected with velocity $20 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ at $60^{\circ}$ with horizontal. The decrease in kinetic energy of the ball during the motion from point of projection to highest point is
question: A ball of mass 100 g is projected with velocity $20 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ at $60^{\circ}$ with horizontal. The decrease in kinetic energy of the ball during the motion from point of projection to highest point is
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{k}_{\mathrm{i}}=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{mv}^2 \\ & \mathrm{k}_{\mathrm{f}}=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{~m}\left(\mathrm{v} \cos 60^{\circ}\right)^2=\frac{1}{8} m v^2 \\ & \Delta \mathrm{k}=\mathrm{k}_{\mathrm{i}}-\mathrm{k}_{\mathrm{f}}=\frac{3}{8} \mathrm{mv}^2=\frac{3}{8} \times 0.1 \times 400=15 \mathrm{~J}\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: A body is projected at $t=0$ with a velocity $10 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$ at an angle of $60^{\circ}$ with the horizontal. The radius of curvature of its trajectory at $t=1$ s is $R$. Neglecting air resistance and taking acceleration due to gravity $\mathrm{g}=10 \mathrm{~ms}^{-2}$, the value of $R$ is:
question: A body is projected at $t=0$ with a velocity $10 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$ at an angle of $60^{\circ}$ with the horizontal. The radius of curvature of its trajectory at $t=1$ s is $R$. Neglecting air resistance and taking acceleration due to gravity $\mathrm{g}=10 \mathrm{~ms}^{-2}$, the value of $R$ is:
solution: Horizontal component of velocity $\mathrm{v}_{x}=10 \cos 60^{\circ}=5 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ vertical component of velocity $v_{y}=10 \cos 30^{\circ}=5 \sqrt{3} \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ After $\mathrm{t}=1 \mathrm{sec}$ Horizontal component of velocity $\mathrm{v}_{x}=5 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ Vertical component of velocity $v_{y}=|(5 \sqrt{3}-10)| \mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}=10-5 \sqrt{3}$ Centripetal, acceleration $\mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{n}}=\frac{\mathrm{v}^{2}}{\mathrm{R}}$ $\Rightarrow \mathrm{R}=\frac{\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{x}}^{2}+\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{y}}^{2}}{\mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{n}}}=\frac{25+100+75-100 \sqrt{3}}{10 \cos \theta}$ From figure (using (i)) $\tan \theta=\frac{10-5 \sqrt{3}}{5}=2-\sqrt{3} \Rightarrow \theta=15^{\circ}$ $\mathrm{R}=\frac{100(2-\sqrt{3})}{10 \cos 15}=2.8 \mathrm{~m}$

q: At time <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> a particle starts travelling from a height <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>7</mn><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">z</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> in a plane keeping <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>z</mi></math> coordinate constant. At <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> acceleration of the particle will be
question: At time <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> a particle starts travelling from a height <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>7</mn><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">z</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> in a plane keeping <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>z</mi></math> coordinate constant. At any instant of time, it's position along the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi></math> directions are defined as <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mi>t</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><msup><mi>t</mi><mn>3</mn></msup></math> respectively. At <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> acceleration of the particle will be
solution:
The position vector of the particle can be written as,

r→=3ti^+5t3j^+7k^

The velocity of the particle will be, 

v→=dr→dt=3i^+15t2j^

Now the acceleration of the particle will be,

a→=dv→dt=d2r→dt2=30tj^

At t=1 s⇒d2rdt2=30j^

subject: Physics
q: The trajectory of a projectile in a vertical plane is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>α</mi><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mi>β</mi><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>,</mo></math> where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>β</mi></math> are constants and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo> </mo><mo>&</mo><mo> </mo><mi>y</mi></math> are respectively the horizontal and vertical distances of the projectile from the point of projection. The angle of projection <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>θ</mi></math> and the maximum height attained <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>H</mi></math> are respectively given by
question: The trajectory of a projectile in a vertical plane is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>α</mi><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mi>β</mi><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>,</mo></math> where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>β</mi></math> are constants and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo> </mo><mo>&</mo><mo> </mo><mi>y</mi></math> are respectively the horizontal and vertical distances of the projectile from the point of projection. The angle of projection <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>θ</mi></math> and the maximum height attained <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>H</mi></math> are respectively given by
solution:
y=αx-βx2
comparing with trajectory equation

y=xtanθ-12gx2u2cos2θ

tanθ=α⇒θ=tan-1α

β=12gu2cos2θ

u2=g2βcos2θ

Maximum height : H

H=u2sin2θ2 g=g2βcos2θsin2θ2 g

H=tan2θ4β=α24β

subject: Physics
q: A particle of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi></math> projected with a velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>u</mi></math> making an angle of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mo>°</mo></math> with the horizontal. The magnitude of angular momentum of the projectile about the point of projection when the particle is at its maximum height <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>h</mi></math> is :
question: A particle of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi></math> projected with a velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>u</mi></math> making an angle of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mo>°</mo></math> with the horizontal. The magnitude of angular momentum of the projectile about the point of projection when the particle is at its maximum height <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>h</mi></math> is :
solution:
Angular momentum at maximum height will be, L=mucosθh, where h=maximum height

Therefore, L=mucosθ×u2sin2θ2g

=mu32g×32×122=3mu316g

subject: Physics
q: A particle starts from the origin at <math><mi>t</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> with an initial velocity of <math><mn>3.0</mn><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>i</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover><mi></mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo>/</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> and moves in the <math><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mi>y</mi></math> plane with a constant acceleration <math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>6.0</mn><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>i</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mn>4.0</mn><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>j</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover></mrow></mfenced><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo>/</mo><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>.</mo></math> The <math><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo></math> coordinate of the particle at the instant when its <math><mi>y</mi><mo>-</mo></math> coordinate is <math><mn>32</mn><mi></mi><mi>m</mi></math> is <math><mi>D</mi></math> meters. The value of <math><mi>D</mi></math> is:
question: A particle starts from the origin at <math><mi>t</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> with an initial velocity of <math><mn>3.0</mn><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>i</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover><mi></mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo>/</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> and moves in the <math><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mi>y</mi></math> plane with a constant acceleration <math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>6.0</mn><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>i</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mn>4.0</mn><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>j</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover></mrow></mfenced><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo>/</mo><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>.</mo></math> The <math><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo></math> coordinate of the particle at the instant when its <math><mi>y</mi><mo>-</mo></math> coordinate is <math><mn>32</mn><mi></mi><mi>m</mi></math> is <math><mi>D</mi></math> meters. The value of <math><mi>D</mi></math> is:
solution:
Sy=uyt+12ayt2
32=0+12×4t2⇒t=4 s
Sx=uxt+12axt2
=3×4+12×6×16
=60 m

subject: Physics
q: Two projectiles <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> are thrown with initial velocities of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>40</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> at angles <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mo>°</mo></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mo>°</mo></math> with the horizontal respectively. The ratio of their ranges respectively is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo> </mo><mo>=</mo><mo> </mo><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>)</mo></math>
question: Two projectiles <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> are thrown with initial velocities of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>40</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> at angles <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mo>°</mo></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mo>°</mo></math> with the horizontal respectively. The ratio of their ranges respectively is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo> </mo><mo>=</mo><mo> </mo><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>)</mo></math>
solution:
The range R1 of the first projectile is given by

R1=u12sin2θ1g   ...(1)

The range R2 of the second projectile is given by

R2=u22sin2θ2g   ...(2)

Divide equation (1) by equation (2) and simplify to obtain the ratio of the ranges.

R1R2=u12sin2θ1gu22sin2θ2g= u12sin2θ1u22sin2θ2   ...3

Substitute the values of the known parameters into equation (3) to calculate the required ratio.

 R1R2=402×sin60°602×sin120°=49

subject: Physics
q: A boy can throw a stone up to a maximum height of $10 \mathrm{~m}$. The maximum horizontal distance that the boy can throw the same stone up to will be
question: A boy can throw a stone up to a maximum height of $10 \mathrm{~m}$. The maximum horizontal distance that the boy can throw the same stone up to will be
solution:
maximum vertical height $=\frac{u^2}{2 g}=10 \mathrm{~m}$
Horizontal range of a projectile $=\frac{u^2 \sin 2 \theta}{\mathrm{g}}$
Range is maximum when $\theta=45^{\circ}$
Maximum horizontal range $=\frac{\mathrm{u}^2}{\mathrm{~g}}$
Hence maximum horizontal distance $=20 \mathrm{~m}$.

subject: Physics
q: A particle is projected from a point $\mathrm{O}$ with velocity $u$ at an angle of $60^{\circ}$ with the horizontal. When it is moving in a direction at right angles to its direction at $O$, its velocity then is given by
question: A particle is projected from a point $\mathrm{O}$ with velocity $u$ at an angle of $60^{\circ}$ with the horizontal. When it is moving in a direction at right angles to its direction at $O$, its velocity then is given by
solution: $\mathrm{u} \cos 60^{\circ}=\mathrm{v} \cos 30^{\circ}$ $$ v=\frac{4}{\sqrt{3}} $$

subject: Physics
q: A small bob tied at one end of a thin string of length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> is describing a vertical circle so that the maximum and minimum tension in the string is in the ratio <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo>:</mo><mn>1</mn></math>. The velocity of the bob at the highest position is ______ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. (Take <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>)
question: A small bob tied at one end of a thin string of length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> is describing a vertical circle so that the maximum and minimum tension in the string is in the ratio <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo>:</mo><mn>1</mn></math>. The velocity of the bob at the highest position is ______ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. (Take <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>)
solution:
Let the speed of bob at lowest position be v1 and at the highest position be v2.

Maximum tension is at lowest position and minimum tension is at the highest position.
Now, using, conservation of mechanical energy,

12mv12=12mv22+mg2l

⇒  v12=v22+4 gl   ....1

Now, Tmax-mg=mv12l

⇒ Tmax=mg+mv12l

&  Tmin+mg=mv22l

⇒ Tmin=mv22l-mg

TmaxTmin=51

⇒  mg+mv12lmv22l-mg=51

⇒ mg+mv12l=mv22l-mg5

⇒ mg+mlv22+4 gl=5mv22l-5mg

⇒ mg+mv22l+4mg=5mv22l-5mg

⇒ 10 mg=4mv22l

v2=10×10×14

Thus, the velocity of the bob at the highest position is 5 m s-1.

subject: Physics
q: A river of width 200 m is flowing from west to east with a speed of $18 \mathrm{~km} / \mathrm{h}$. A boat, moving with speed of $36 \mathrm{~km} / \mathrm{h}$ in still water, is made to travel one-round trip (bank to bank of the river). Minimum time taken by the boat for this journey and also the displacement along the river bank are $\_\_\_\_$ and $\_\_\_\_$ respectively.
question: A river of width 200 m is flowing from west to east with a speed of $18 \mathrm{~km} / \mathrm{h}$. A boat, moving with speed of $36 \mathrm{~km} / \mathrm{h}$ in still water, is made to travel one-round trip (bank to bank of the river). Minimum time taken by the boat for this journey and also the displacement along the river bank are $\_\_\_\_$ and $\_\_\_\_$ respectively.
solution:
Width of the river $d = 200$ m.
Speed of river $v_r = 18$ km/h $= 18 \times \frac{5}{18} = 5$ m/s.
Speed of boat in still water $v_b = 36$ km/h $= 36 \times \frac{5}{18} = 10$ m/s.
For minimum time to cross the river, the boat must head perpendicular to the river flow (at $90^\circ$ to the bank).
Time taken to cross once $t = \frac{d}{v_b} = \frac{200}{10} = 20$ s.
For a round trip (bank to bank and back), the total minimum time is $T = 2t = 2 \times 20 = 40$ s.
During the first crossing, the drift (displacement along the bank) is $x_1 = v_r \times t = 5 \times 20 = 100$ m.
During the return trip, to minimize time, the boat again heads perpendicular to the flow relative to the water. The river continues to flow in the same direction, so the drift is again in the same direction as the first trip.
Return drift $x_2 = v_r \times t = 5 \times 20 = 100$ m.
Total displacement along the river bank $= x_1 + x_2 = 100 + 100 = 200$ m.
Thus, minimum time is $40$ s and displacement is $200$ m.

subject: Physics
q: A body of mass $M$ thrown horizontally with velocity $v$ from the top of the tower of height $H$ touches the ground at a distance of $100 \mathrm{~m}$ from the foot of the tower. A body of mass $2 \mathrm{M}$ thrown at a velocity $\frac{v}{2}$ from the top of the tower of height $4 \mathrm{H}$ will touch the ground at a distance of _____$\mathrm{m}$.
question: A body of mass $M$ thrown horizontally with velocity $v$ from the top of the tower of height $H$ touches the ground at a distance of $100 \mathrm{~m}$ from the foot of the tower. A body of mass $2 \mathrm{M}$ thrown at a velocity $\frac{v}{2}$ from the top of the tower of height $4 \mathrm{H}$ will touch the ground at a distance of _____$\mathrm{m}$.
solution: $\begin{aligned} & 100=v \sqrt{\frac{2 \mathrm{H}}{\mathrm{g}}} ; \quad \mathrm{x}=\frac{v}{2} \sqrt{\frac{2(4 \mathrm{H})}{\mathrm{g}}}=v \sqrt{\frac{2 \mathrm{H}}{\mathrm{g}}} \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{x}=100\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: A man carrying a monkey on his shoulder does cycling smoothly on a circular track of radius $9 \mathrm{~m}$ and completes 120 resolutions in 3 minutes. The magnitude of centripetal acceleration of monkey is $\left(\right.$ in $\mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}^2$ ) :
question: A man carrying a monkey on his shoulder does cycling smoothly on a circular track of radius $9 \mathrm{~m}$ and completes 120 resolutions in 3 minutes. The magnitude of centripetal acceleration of monkey is $\left(\right.$ in $\mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}^2$ ) :
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \text { Given : } \mathrm{R}=9 \mathrm{~m}, \\ & 120 \text { revolution in } 3 \mathrm{~min} \\ & \omega=\frac{120 \mathrm{Rev} .}{3 \mathrm{~min} .}=\frac{120 \times 2 \pi \mathrm{rad}}{3 \times 60 \mathrm{sec}}=\frac{4 \pi}{3} \mathrm{rad} / \mathrm{s} \\ & \mathrm{a}_{\text {centripetal }}=\omega^2 \mathrm{R}=\left(\frac{4 \pi}{3}\right)^2 \times 9=16 \pi^2 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: The ranges and heights for two projectiles projected with the same initial velocity at angles <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>42</mn><mo>°</mo></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>48</mn><mo>°</mo></math> with the horizontal are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>R</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><msub><mi>R</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>H</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><msub><mi>H</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> respectively. Choose the correct option:
question: The ranges and heights for two projectiles projected with the same initial velocity at angles <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>42</mn><mo>°</mo></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>48</mn><mo>°</mo></math> with the horizontal are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>R</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><msub><mi>R</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>H</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><msub><mi>H</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> respectively. Choose the correct option:
solution: Range<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mrow><msup><mi>u</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mi>sin</mi><mn>2</mn><mi>θ</mi></mrow><mi>g</mi></mfrac></math> and same for <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>θ</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>90</mn><mo>-</mo><mi>θ</mi></math>. So, same for <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>42</mn><mo>°</mo></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>48</mn><mo>°</mo></math> Maximum height <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>H</mi><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mrow><msup><mi>u</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><msup><mi>sin</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mi>θ</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn><mi>g</mi></mrow></mfrac></math>, so height is high for higher <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>θ</mi></math>. So, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>H</mi></math> for <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>48</mn><mo>°</mo></math> is higher than <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>H</mi></math> for <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>42</mn><mo>°</mo></math>.

subject: Physics
q: A particle is projected with velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>v</mi><mn>0</mn></msub></math> along <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math>-axis. A damping force is acting on the particle which is proportional to the square of the distance from the origin i.e. <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi><mi>a</mi><mo>=</mo><mo>-</mo><mi>α</mi><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>.</mo></math> The distance at which the particle stops:
question: A particle is projected with velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>v</mi><mn>0</mn></msub></math> along <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math>-axis. A damping force is acting on the particle which is proportional to the square of the distance from the origin i.e. <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi><mi>a</mi><mo>=</mo><mo>-</mo><mi>α</mi><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>.</mo></math> The distance at which the particle stops:
solution:
F=-αx2

ma=-αx2

a=-αx2 m

vdvdx=-αmx2

∫v00vdv=∫0x-αmx2dx

v22v00=-αmx330x

-v022=-αmx33

x=3mv022α13

subject: Physics
q: A projectile is thrown upward at an angle $60^{\circ}$ with the horizontal. The speed of the projectile is $20 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ when its direction of motion is $45^{\circ}$ with the horizontal. The initial speed of the projectile is $\_\_\_\_$ $\mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}$.
question: A projectile is thrown upward at an angle $60^{\circ}$ with the horizontal. The speed of the projectile is $20 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ when its direction of motion is $45^{\circ}$ with the horizontal. The initial speed of the projectile is $\_\_\_\_$ $\mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}$.
solution:
Horizontal velocity is constant: $v_x = u\cos 60° = \frac{u}{2}$.
When direction is $45°$ with horizontal and speed is 20 m/s:
$v_x = 20\cos 45° = \frac{20}{\sqrt{2}}$.
$\frac{u}{2} = \frac{20}{\sqrt{2}} \Rightarrow u = \frac{40}{\sqrt{2}} = 20\sqrt{2}$ m/s.

subject: Physics
q: The angle of projection for a projectile to have same horizontal range and maximum height is :
question: The angle of projection for a projectile to have same horizontal range and maximum height is :
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \frac{\mathrm{u}^2 \sin 2 \theta}{\mathrm{g}}=\frac{\mathrm{u}^2 \sin ^2 \theta}{2 \mathrm{~g}} \\ & 4 \sin \theta \cos \theta=\sin ^2 \theta \\ & 4=\tan \theta\end{aligned}$
